title: Sweetums

jokes.text: Hi, I'm Tom Haverford. I work at the Parks Department. It's a little douchie.
jokes.setup: Standard workplace introduction in talking head
jokes.punchline: Immediately calls his own workplace douchey
jokes.explanation: Good character moment establishing Tom's shallow self-awareness, economical delivery

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Welcome to Parks and Recreation, where we do important work for the city of Pawnee. I'm Tom Haverford, Director of Parks and Leisure. This department is absolutely douchey.

jokes.text: Who am I supposed to ask for fashion advice? Jerry? He wears the same soup-stained khakis every day.
jokes.setup: Tom needs fashion advice
jokes.punchline: Jerry wearing soup-stained khakis as the obvious bad choice
jokes.explanation: Strong character work - establishes both Tom's vanity and Jerry's slovenliness with specific visual detail

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Jerry, what are you wearing?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? These are my good khakis.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Jerry, they're covered in soup stains. That's not fashion advice, that's a cry for help.

jokes.text: Justin's my savior. He's like an issue of GQ that's come to life.
jokes.setup: Tom describing his fashion consultant
jokes.punchline: Overly dramatic metaphor about GQ magazine coming to life
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy showing Tom's fashion obsession and tendency toward hyperbole

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: My fashion consultant is like if GQ magazine came to life and decided to just roast you for two hours straight.

jokes.text: Tommy Timberlake. You look like Encyclopedia Brown.
jokes.setup: Fashion consultant giving Tom a nickname and assessment
jokes.punchline: Comparing fashionable Tom to nerdy boy detective character
jokes.explanation: Sharp pop culture reference that deflates Tom's fashion aspirations with specific, unexpected comparison

dialogue.speaker: Fashion Consultant
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm going to call you 'Champ.' You've got real style, Tom. You're like a young Shaft.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Shaft? Really?

dialogue.speaker: Fashion Consultant
dialogue.line: No, wait. You're more of a Veronica Mars.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Veronica Mars? She's a girl!

dialogue.speaker: Fashion Consultant
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: Hey, what's up? I'm Tom. Is my shirt lighting up? 'Cause I didn't even notice.
jokes.setup: Tom trying to casually introduce himself
jokes.punchline: Pretending not to notice his obviously flashy light-up shirt
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Tom's transparent attempt to seem casual while showing off

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hey, what's up? I'm Tom Haverford. Just, you know, keeping it low-key tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: This old thing? Nah, it's just a regular shirt. Nothing special about it.

jokes.text: Be honest. Which cane do you like better? Dragon? Serpent?
jokes.setup: Tom asking for accessory advice
jokes.punchline: Choosing between decorative canes as fashion accessories
jokes.explanation: Absurd escalation of Tom's fashion obsession - decorative canes are wonderfully ridiculous

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Should I get the mahogany cane or the ebony cane?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Tom, you don't need a cane. You're not old.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: It's not about age, Jerry. It's about style. A cane is a power accessory.

jokes.text: Belt buckle, says, 'What's cracking?' I can have it say whatever I want. It can say, 'What's cracking? I'm Tom. What's cracking, girl? What's cracking, boo?'
jokes.setup: Tom showing off customizable belt buckle
jokes.punchline: Escalating variations of 'What's cracking' getting progressively more awkward
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation structure showing Tom's complete lack of self-awareness about what's cool

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What's cracking?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What's cracking, baby?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What's cracking, sexy mama?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What's cracking, you beautiful son of a gun?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What's cracking, you absolute unit of human perfection?

jokes.text: Well, as far as white, leather suits go... It's horrible. I like it.
jokes.setup: Justin evaluating Tom's white leather suit
jokes.punchline: Brutal honest assessment followed by Tom's oblivious enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/punchline with Justin's honesty contrasting Tom's delusion

dialogue.speaker: Justin
dialogue.line: Tom, I'm going to be brutally honest with you. That suit is not working.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What? No, this is fire. I look like a sexy snowman.

jokes.text: Wow, that family looks so healthy. Look at them. They're all wearing vests!
jokes.setup: Commenting on family in commercial
jokes.punchline: Vests as evidence of health
jokes.explanation: Absurd logic connecting clothing choice to health - unexpected and specific

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: A man in a vest is a man with a plan.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: That's not true at all.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Look at any historical figure of note. Always wearing a vest.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: Most of them were wearing full suits.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: The vest was the important part.

jokes.text: Sorry, shoelace, we don't make nutriyums For dogs... Yet.
jokes.setup: Addressing a dog in commercial
jokes.punchline: The ominous 'yet' suggesting future dog products
jokes.explanation: Subtle corporate menace hiding in cheerful commercial

dialogue.speaker: Nick Newport Jr.
dialogue.line: Does your dog enjoy our delicious Sweetums brand dog food? Well, not yet.

jokes.text: Chuck E. Cheese could run the parks. Everything operated by tokens. Drop in a token, go on the swing set. Drop in another token, take a walk. Drop in a token, look at a duck.
jokes.setup: Ron supporting privatization
jokes.punchline: Escalating absurdity of tokenizing basic park activities like looking at ducks
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation showing Ron's extreme libertarian views taken to absurd logical conclusion

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm not against parks. I'm just saying they'd be more efficient if they were privately run. You could monetize every aspect.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Want to walk on the grass? That'll be fifty cents. Looking at ducks? A dollar. Breathing the fresh air? Two dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Sitting on a bench? That's five dollars. And if you want to actually feed the ducks, well, you need to purchase a special duck-feeding license for twenty-five dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Smiling at nature? That's going to cost you. We'd have vendors everywhere collecting payments. The government couldn't run anything this efficiently.

jokes.text: Brendanawicz! Quick question, do you personally know Xzibit? Because I was checking out that pickup truck of yours, and that ride is pimped!
jokes.setup: Tom complimenting Mark's truck
jokes.punchline: Assuming any nice truck must be connected to Pimp My Ride
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tom's pop culture obsession and shallow thinking

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yo, that's a nice truck, Mark. Did Xzibit pimp it out?

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: What? No. I just... bought it like this.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Mmmhmm, sure you did.

jokes.text: It's really nice of you to compliment my pickup truck with no ulterior motives.
jokes.setup: Tom's obvious flattery
jokes.punchline: Mark's sarcastic acknowledgment of Tom's transparent motives
jokes.explanation: Solid deadpan response that calls out Tom's obvious manipulation

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Mark, you are the most talented, intelligent, and physically attractive man I have ever met.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: What do you want?

jokes.text: You can bring up the Mark-mobile, help me move, right?
jokes.setup: Tom asking Mark to help him move
jokes.punchline: Calling Mark's truck the 'Mark-mobile'
jokes.explanation: Mild character humor showing Tom's casual manipulation and silly naming

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hey Mark, can you help me move? I need to borrow your truck.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Yeah, sure.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Great! We can take the Mark-mobile!

jokes.text: I'm totally blanking on a valid excuse right now, so, yes. I'll help you.
jokes.setup: Mark being asked to help Tom move
jokes.punchline: Honest admission he can't think of an excuse
jokes.explanation: Good honest humor - Mark's straightforward admission of his predicament

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: I'd love to help Tom move, but I can't think of an excuse.

jokes.text: I (fucking) hate having a pickup truck.
jokes.setup: Mark just agreed to help Tom move
jokes.punchline: Confessional revealing his true feelings about truck ownership
jokes.explanation: Perfect talking head beat - relatable frustration expressed with economy

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: I agreed to help Tom move. I didn't think about it. I just said yes. Now I'm realizing I have to spend my Saturday helping Tom move into some apartment that probably has a weird smell and is filled with his stupid furniture that he bought at some discount warehouse.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: And of course Tom's going to want to borrow my truck. Everyone always wants to borrow my truck. 'Hey Mark, can I use your truck? Hey Mark, my truck's in the shop, can I borrow yours?' No. Get your own truck.

jokes.text: Afterwards, I'll take the cardboard from the boxes and use it for break dancing.
jokes.setup: Andy volunteering to help move
jokes.punchline: His motivation is getting cardboard for breakdancing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy character moment - childlike enthusiasm for something completely random

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'll help you move! I'm really strong and I've got great cardio from all my breakdancing.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Plus, you're gonna have all those cardboard boxes lying around, right? That's basically free breakdancing equipment.

jokes.text: 'Cause an hour ago you told me you'd rather watch a sex tape of your grandparents.
jokes.setup: April suddenly volunteering to help
jokes.punchline: Revealing how much April initially didn't want to help
jokes.explanation: Dark humor showing April's previous reluctance and Andy's blunt honesty

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: April, you volunteering? That's amazing!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Yeah, well... I'm here now.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This is so great! What made you want to help?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Ben said if I didn't come, he'd tell everyone about the time I cried at a dog food commercial.

jokes.text: I think that that's really, really sweet, that your grandparents still make love.
jokes.setup: Previous joke about grandparents' sex tape
jokes.punchline: Tom completely missing the point and finding it romantic
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Tom's obliviousness and misplaced romantic notions

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's actually really sweet. Your grandparents still have that spark, you know? I think that's beautiful. I hope me and my future wife are still doing that when we're old.

jokes.text: What are you, 5'11", 210 pounds, three whiskeys? Looks like you are just over the line into impaired.
jokes.setup: Leslie checking if Ron can drive
jokes.punchline: Precise calculation with a chart for blood alcohol
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie character moment showing her over-preparedness and rule-following

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, can you drive?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have a precise calculation. I've made a chart.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A chart of what?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Blood alcohol content versus my ability to operate a vehicle. According to my data, I'm actually a better driver after exactly four whiskeys.

jokes.text: You carry that with you all the time? It comes in handy. And on the back, it teaches you how to play blackjack.
jokes.setup: Ron questioning Leslie's blood alcohol chart
jokes.punchline: Revealing it also has blackjack instructions
jokes.explanation: Absurd efficiency - Leslie has combined safety and gambling instruction on one card

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, what is this chart?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's a blood alcohol content reference guide. You can use it to determine how many drinks you can safely have based on your body weight and—

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I see. And what is this section here at the bottom?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, that's blackjack instructions.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Why would you put blackjack instructions on a blood alcohol chart?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Because if you're drunk enough to need this chart, you're probably drunk enough to want to play blackjack.

jokes.text: Swansons have a preternaturally high tolerance for alcohol. My old man used to put Wild Turkey on his Corn Flakes.
jokes.setup: Ron explaining his alcohol tolerance
jokes.punchline: His father putting whiskey on cereal
jokes.explanation: Excellent Ron character moment - specific, absurd family history that feels authentic to character

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: My father put whiskey on my cereal when I was a boy.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's why I can drink so much without getting drunk.

jokes.text: After I got home, I drank six more glasses of whiskey and then I finished crafting this small harp, using a band saw, a spoke shave and an oscillating spindle sander.
jokes.setup: Ron showing what he made after drinking
jokes.punchline: Precise craftsmanship requiring specialized tools while heavily intoxicated
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Ron character moment - combines his drinking, craftsmanship, and attention to detail in one absurd beat

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I made this after drinking an entire bottle of scotch.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's a birdhouse. Dovetail joints, mortise and tenon construction, hand-planed wood.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have no idea how I did that.

jokes.text: Here are some photographs of me drinking the whiskey. You'll notice I'm holding up yesterday's newspaper, so you can tell that I'm not lying.
jokes.setup: Ron providing evidence of his drinking
jokes.punchline: Treating it like a hostage proof-of-life photo
jokes.explanation: Genius escalation - applying kidnapping photo conventions to prove alcohol consumption

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have nothing to hide. Here's photographic evidence of my drinking.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Today's newspaper. My whiskey. My face. Proof of life.

jokes.text: I'm not a mover! I'm a sap who owns a truck.
jokes.setup: Tom expecting Mark to have moving equipment
jokes.punchline: Mark's self-aware frustrated description
jokes.explanation: Perfect economical expression of Mark's truck-owner frustration

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Mark, where's all your moving equipment? Dolly, hand truck, something?

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: I don't own a dolly, Tom. I'm a 40-year-old man who lives alone and has accepted that.

jokes.text: Terrific's not more than amazing, Jerry. No? Well, it's not less.
jokes.setup: Jerry calling energy bars terrific
jokes.punchline: Andy correcting him, Jerry's confused logic
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jerry's confused logic and Andy's literalness

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: These energy bars are terrific!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Actually, they're terrible, Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, I'm pretty sure they're terrific.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Jerry, terrific means good.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know what terrific means, Andy. And these bars are it.

jokes.text: Krackel bars, also healthy and delicious. No, they're not. Yeah. They actually have rice in them. So...
jokes.setup: Jerry claiming Krackel bars are healthy
jokes.punchline: His logic that rice makes candy healthy
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat showing Jerry's flawed logic about nutrition

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Krackel bars are basically healthy. They're mostly rice.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Rice doesn't make candy healthy, Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, it should.

jokes.text: Andy, you're fine, but you're simple.
jokes.setup: Andy making naive comments about nutrition
jokes.punchline: Ann's blunt but kind assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - captures Andy's character and Ann's directness in one line

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy, you can't just eat bacon and think it's a vegetable because it comes from a pig and pigs eat vegetables.

jokes.text: Ann! Oh! Look at you! You're looking pretty unhealthy today. You might need one of these, NutriYum, by Sweetums.
jokes.setup: Tom trying to sell Ann energy bars
jokes.punchline: Insulting her appearance as a sales technique
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing Tom's inappropriate sales approach

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ann, these energy bars are gonna change your life. They're packed with nutrients, they taste amazing, and they'll give you the energy you need to get through the day.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Tom, I'm not really interested in energy bars.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Well, that's the thing. Looking at you, I'd say you could use all the help you can get. These bars will really... improve things. Visually, I mean. For you.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Are you seriously trying to insult me into buying your energy bars?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Is it working?

jokes.text: No, they're not. There's a picture of Lance Armstrong on there. Yeah, but look at the ingredients.
jokes.setup: Leslie defending energy bars as healthy
jokes.punchline: Using celebrity endorsement as proof of healthiness
jokes.explanation: Solid joke about celebrity endorsement logic vs actual ingredients

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: These energy bars are completely healthy. They have all the nutrients you need.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Leslie, they're 90% sugar.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, Oprah endorsed them, so...

jokes.text: Generally, I like to stay out of other people's business. But Pawnee is the fourth most obese city in America. The kids here are beefy. They're just husky, big-boned, plus-sized chunk monsters. I call them like I see them.
jokes.setup: Ann explaining why she's getting involved
jokes.punchline: Escalating euphemisms ending with 'chunk monsters'
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of politically correct terms ending with brutal honesty

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm getting involved because these issues matter to me.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: We need to address the challenges, the obstacles, the complications, the difficulties, the problems, and frankly, the chunk monsters in this community.

jokes.text: You're pretty cocky for someone whose job is obsolete because of the Internet.
jokes.setup: Librarian being snarky to Leslie
jokes.punchline: Leslie's comeback about libraries being obsolete
jokes.explanation: Good comeback that's topical and sharp

dialogue.speaker: Librarian
dialogue.line: Well, actually, libraries are still very important institutions.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh really? Because the last time I checked, Google exists. So unless you're planning to alphabetize the entire internet, I think we're good.

jokes.text: You seem to have a $40 late fee on a book called Mysteries of the Female Orgasm.
jokes.setup: Librarian looking up Leslie's account
jokes.punchline: Embarrassing book title announced publicly
jokes.explanation: Standard embarrassing library book joke - functional but not original

dialogue.speaker: Marcia
dialogue.line: Oh my, someone checked out 'How to Identify and Treat Hemorrhoids'.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's not mine!

jokes.text: Punk-ass book jockeys!
jokes.setup: Leslie and Ann fleeing the library
jokes.punchline: Leslie's angry insult toward librarians
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Leslie's indignation expressed in unexpected terminology

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know what? Forget the Dewey Decimal System! It's more like the Dewey Decimal Nightmare!

jokes.text: I care about the people's right to consume whatever they want.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking if Ron cares about city health
jokes.punchline: Ron's libertarian response prioritizing freedom over health
jokes.explanation: Perfect character consistency - Ron's libertarian philosophy in action

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, don't you care about the health and well-being of our citizens?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: No. I don't care about people's health. And I don't think the government should be involved in people's health decisions. If someone wants to smoke a cigarette, they should be allowed to smoke a cigarette. If someone wants to drink a beer, they should be allowed to drink a beer. The government has no right to tell people what to do.

jokes.text: Sidebar, Paul. Ron's upset because I didn't let him drive last night.
jokes.setup: Paul asking why Leslie keeps calling sidebars
jokes.punchline: Revealing the petty personal reason behind professional tension
jokes.explanation: Good callback connecting personal and professional conflicts

dialogue.speaker: Paul
dialogue.line: Why do you keep calling sidebars?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Because you keep doing things that require sidebars!

dialogue.speaker: Paul
dialogue.line: What things?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know what things. You wore a turtleneck to my house.

jokes.text: Dude, Deep Blue Sea. Greatest movie ever made. That's the Canadian version, 22 extra minutes, and there's a bonus audio track where LL Cool J raps all his dialog.
jokes.setup: Tom showing off a movie
jokes.punchline: Absurd details about a Canadian version with rapped dialogue
jokes.explanation: Absurd specific details that feel like something Tom would genuinely be excited about

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: So there's this Canadian version of the movie where all the dialogue is rapped.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: It's called 'The Maple Leaf Remix.'

jokes.text: No, it's a spare room I converted into a walk-in closet/home fitness center.
jokes.setup: Someone asking if this is Tom's bedroom
jokes.punchline: Revealing the over-the-top nature of his living space
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing Tom's priorities and lifestyle excess

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Welcome to my bedroom. As you can see, I've spared no expense. Italian marble floors, a bed made from the finest Egyptian cotton, and a closet that's basically a small department store.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I also have a sauna, a steam room, and a personal masseuse on call 24/7. And that's just the entryway.

jokes.text: I strapped an MP3 player to one of those floor cleaning robots. I call him 'DJ Roomba.'
jokes.setup: Tom explaining a device in his room
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous concept and name of DJ Roomba
jokes.explanation: Instant classic - perfectly absurd invention that becomes a running gag

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: This is DJ Roomba. It's a Roomba with a mini DJ setup on top of it. So it cleans your floor while simultaneously DJing your party.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I've been working on the name. I'm thinking either 'The Dust- turner' or 'Roombarton Barton'... no, I like DJ Roomba better.

jokes.text: What's hot, DJ Roomba? DJ Roomba, tearing it up!
jokes.setup: Tom introducing DJ Roomba
jokes.punchline: Talking to the robot like a real DJ
jokes.explanation: Good extension of the DJ Roomba concept showing Tom's genuine enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yo, yo, yo, check it out. We got DJ Roomba in the house!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's what I'm talking about. Keep it spinning, baby. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

jokes.text: The whole point of this country is, if you want to eat garbage, balloon up to 600 pounds and die of a heart attack at 43, you can. You are free to do so. To me, that's beautiful.
jokes.setup: Ron defending people's right to eat unhealthily
jokes.punchline: Finding death by poor nutrition choices 'beautiful' as freedom
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron moment - extreme libertarianism taken to darkly comic logical conclusion

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I think it's beautiful. People have the right to make their own choices about what they put in their bodies, even if those choices lead to their death.

jokes.text: If sugar is so bad, how come Jesus made it taste so good?
jokes.setup: Ann explaining sugar's health risks
jokes.punchline: Religious justification for sugar's taste
jokes.explanation: Good public forum logic - absurd religious reasoning for dietary choices

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Sugar is terrible for you. It rots your teeth, it makes you fat, it causes diabetes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But it tastes so good!

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That's because God wants you to die.

jokes.text: What's so bad about corn syrup? It's natural. Corn's a fruit. Syrup comes from a bush.
jokes.setup: Citizen questioning corn syrup's dangers
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong botanical classifications
jokes.explanation: Solid public ignorance joke showing flawed reasoning about natural vs artificial

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: Is corn syrup really that bad for you?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, actually, corn syrup comes from corn, which is a vegetable, so it's basically health food.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Corn isn't a vegetable, Jerry. It's a grain.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm pretty sure corn is a fruit.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: It's definitely not a fruit, Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, what about corn on the cob? That's basically a vegetable.

jokes.text: Well, the point is, my friend thinks you're cute. Give me your number so he can have it.
jokes.setup: Citizen at health meeting
jokes.punchline: Using public forum to hit on the nurse
jokes.explanation: Basic cringe comedy - inappropriate behavior in professional setting

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: I just wanted to say that you have really nice... um... healthcare policies.

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: And I think we should all get together sometime. For health reasons.

jokes.text: I think we ought to throw those bars out and eat ham-and-mayonnaise sandwiches. Ham and mayonnaise!
jokes.setup: Discussion about healthy alternatives
jokes.punchline: Suggesting something obviously unhealthier, then chanting it
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation showing how public meetings can devolve into chanting

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: What about something healthier?

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Bacon.

dialogue.speaker: Multiple Citizens
dialogue.line: Bacon! Bacon! Bacon!

jokes.text: Uh-uh. That's it. My baby has a delicate suspension. Put it in your truck, Brendanawicz.
jokes.setup: Moving day with multiple vehicles
jokes.punchline: Donna protecting her car and dumping work on Mark
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Donna's self-interest and Mark's continued truck burden

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Nobody touches my car. Nobody. I don't care if we're moving to a new office or a new planet.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Donna, we need to coordinate the trucks. Can you help organize—

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Mark, that's what you're here for. I'm making sure my Lexus gets there in one piece. You handle the rest.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: But I have to load my own desk and—

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Mark. My car. Your problem. Figure it out.

jokes.text: Cheap, delicious, sugary corn is currently being used by farms to fatten up their cows and pigs. Look how fat those cows are! Fat, happy and docile, the way we like them.
jokes.setup: Old Sweetums promotional video about corn syrup
jokes.punchline: Explicitly connecting animal fattening to human consumption
jokes.explanation: Dark corporate honesty that accidentally reveals sinister intentions

dialogue.speaker: Nick Newport Sr.
dialogue.line: Here at Sweetums, we've perfected the art of animal fattening. We take lean livestock and through the power of corn syrup, we transform them into plump, juicy specimens ready for your dinner table. It's the same process we use on our consumers. I mean, customers.

jokes.text: No, we just thought it'd be funny to see April doing physical labor.
jokes.setup: Friends showing up at Tom's move
jokes.punchline: Honest admission they came to watch April work
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing friends' mean-spirited entertainment

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Dude, we're not here to help you move.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What? Why are you here then?

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: We came to watch April tell you what a terrible job you're doing.

jokes.text: Why don't you get some Canadian bacon on it, since you're from Canada. And sausage, 'cause I am brown and spicy.
jokes.setup: Tom ordering pizza for the movers
jokes.punchline: Awkward self-description as 'brown and spicy'
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe showing Tom's inappropriate self-characterization

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'd like to order a large pizza. And, uh, make sure it's brown and spicy... like me.

dialogue.speaker: Pizza Worker
dialogue.line: ...What?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Just put extra pepperoni on it.

jokes.text: Denver, you little son of a bitch.
jokes.setup: Nick's son disrupts the meeting with free candy
jokes.punchline: Father calling his young child a 'son of a bitch'
jokes.explanation: Dark humor in inappropriate parental language at public event

dialogue.speaker: Nick Newport Sr.
dialogue.line: Son of a bitch!

jokes.text: Jerry! You stepped on DJ Roomba! You killed him! I built him myself. He was like a son to me.
jokes.setup: Jerry accidentally steps on Tom's robot
jokes.punchline: Tom's dramatic overreaction treating robot like family
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Tom's attachment to his ridiculous invention

jokes.text: All those who would like the bloated corpse of the government to keep running the snack bars at taxpayer expense, raise your hands. And all those in favor of letting this heartless corporation, Sweetums, stuff your children with sugary crap, raise your hand.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to conduct a fair vote
jokes.punchline: Completely biased phrasing of both options
jokes.explanation: Good satire of biased survey questions and Leslie's inability to be neutral

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, everyone, we're going to have a completely fair and unbiased vote. Option one: we adopt my plan, which is brilliant and will save the town. Option two: we do nothing and let the town die.

jokes.text: Everybody go home, pack away my stuff in your own houses. Then on Monday, pack it back up, meet me at my new place, unload it there.
jokes.setup: Tom's moving plans falling apart
jokes.punchline: Proposing even more ridiculous solution involving multiple moves
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Tom's obliviousness to how ridiculous his request is

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I've got it. What if we move to London for six months, then to Paris for three months, then back to Pawnee, but just for weekends?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's the worst idea I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Or—or we could move to a boat. We'd just be constantly moving. Problem solved.

jokes.text: Just chillaxin' like your new bro, Andy. Yeah. Wanna play some Hacky Sack later, maybe listen to Blues Traveler?
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio and friend mocking Andy
jokes.punchline: Stereotypical 'bro' activities and dated references
jokes.explanation: Basic stereotype humor - functional but not particularly creative

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Dude, Andy's like, so not chill. He probably listens to like, Nickelback or something.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Yeah bro, and I bet he does like, CrossFit or goes to SoulCycle or something lame like that.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Dude, he probably thinks Silly Bandz are still cool. What a loser.

jokes.text: You know, for a gay couple, you guys are being really gay.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio and friend being mean to Andy
jokes.punchline: Andy's confused logic about gayness
jokes.explanation: Character moment showing Andy's confused attempt at an insult that doesn't make sense

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. So you're saying that if I sleep with a man, that makes me gay?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But what if I think about a woman the whole time?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wouldn't that make it straight?

jokes.text: I call this 'turf and turf.' It's a 16-ounce T-bone and a 24-ounce porterhouse. Also, whiskey and a cigar.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking what Ron is eating
jokes.punchline: Extreme meat-only meal with alcohol and tobacco
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Ron moment - perfectly excessive response to health concerns, great wordplay on 'surf and turf'

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What are you eating?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Meat and eggs. Bacon, steak, a whole roasted chicken. And I'm washing it down with Scotch and a cigar.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's disgusting.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's called the Swanson. I invented it. You can't have any.

jokes.text: By the way, maybe it's none of my business, but if you eat three pounds of steak every day, you're gonna die, and although I've already written your eulogy, and it's incredibly touching, I would prefer not to give it for a while.
jokes.setup: Leslie confronting Ron about his eating
jokes.punchline: Revealing she's already written his eulogy but admitting it's good
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie character moment - over-preparedness meeting genuine care and dark humor

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, you can't keep eating like this. You're going to die.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's fine.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's not fine! I've actually already written your eulogy.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Well, let's hear it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's really good.

jokes.text: 'O Captain! My Captain! Ron Swanson, a swan song.' Yeah, and it gets better from there.
jokes.setup: Leslie mentioning Ron's eulogy
jokes.punchline: Terrible pun on Swanson/swan song with proud delivery
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's wordplay enthusiasm and over-preparation

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron's eulogy was basically a swan song. Or as I like to call it... a Swanson song.

jokes.text: You wanna watch me rollerblade in the parking lot after work? I'm pretty awesome. I used to be really good. That was, like, 70 pounds ago.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking April to hang out
jokes.punchline: Self-aware acknowledgment of weight gain affecting his rollerblading
jokes.explanation: Good Jerry character moment - self-deprecating humor with surprising self-awareness

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Hey April, want to go rollerblading with me this weekend?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Come on, it'll be fun. I used to be really good at it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm a little heavier now, so I mostly just... roll downhill and try to remember how to stop.

jokes.text: Just kidding! I don't fall!
jokes.setup: Jerry claiming he doesn't fall while rollerblading
jokes.punchline: Presumably falling immediately after saying this
jokes.explanation: Classic physical comedy setup - predictable but well-executed for Jerry's character

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't fall. I'm very coordinated on rollerblades.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: *falls*

jokes.text: This is the ghost of DJ Roomba. Why did you kill me? I'm gonna haunt you, Jerry. I'm gonna follow you and play the Black Eyed Peas on a non-stop loop!
jokes.setup: Tom getting revenge on Jerry for breaking DJ Roomba
jokes.punchline: Threatening to haunt Jerry with bad music
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback with escalation - Tom's dramatic revenge with perfect musical torture choice

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Jerry, you broke DJ Roomba. So here's what's gonna happen. When I die, I'm gonna come back and haunt you. And you know what I'm gonna do? I'm gonna follow you around playing nothing but Nickelback for all eternity.

tmdb_overview: The Parks Department forms a partnership with a local company to sell energy bars at the parks in Pawnee, but Leslie tries to stop the deal when she discovers how unhealthy the snacks are. Mark reluctantly agrees to help Tom move out of his house.
insight: Sweetums corporation subplot drives 61 jokes at 2.53-per-minute pace, elevating mid-season form.
